title: Name the reference: ratios, proportions, and percentages

objectives.statement: Interpret an ordered ratio in context, preserve its labels, and generate equivalent ratios.
Solve proportional relationships by identifying a constant multiplier and verify the solution by substitution.
Calculate a part, whole, or percentage while naming the denominator that defines 100 percent.
Distinguish percent change from percentage-point difference and challenge results with bounds or reverse calculations.

scenes.title: Two true percentages that answer different questions
scenes.contentHtml:
Two true percentages that answer different questions

A fictional archive marks 30 of 120 files for rechecking. Later it marks 36 of 120.

  • The marked share rose from 30/120=25%30/120=25\% to 36/120=30%36/120=30\%.
  • That is an increase of 5 percentage points.
  • Relative to the old marked share, the increase is (30%−25%)/25%=20%(30\%-25\%)/25\%=20\%.

Both 5 and 20 are correct, but they answer different questions. The denominator decides the meaning.

This lesson uses a reference-first method: label the compared quantities, name what represents one whole or the starting value, calculate, then reverse or bound the result.

Starting check

Check
id check-starting-reference-skills

Answer all three questions before reading the feedback below.

  1. Does the ratio 3 red : 5 blue mean the same as 5 red : 3 blue?
  2. In “18 is 30% of what number?”, which value represents 100%?
  3. Can a quantity increase by more than 100%?

Starting-check feedback

  1. No. Ratio order preserves the labels: 3 red : 5 blue compares red with blue, while 5 red : 3 blue reverses that comparison.
  2. The unknown whole represents 100%. Writing 18=0.30W18=0.30W makes the reference visible and gives W=60W=60.
  3. Yes. A rise from a positive starting value of 20 to 50 is an increase of 30, and 30/20=150%30/20=150\%.

If fractions are uncertain, read a/ba/b aloud as “a for every b” and attach the labels. If decimal arithmetic is the barrier, use exact fractions until the final conversion.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-percentage-part-whole, claim-percent-change-reference, claim-percentage-point-differencesources source-nist-si-chapter8, source-openstax-prealgebra-ch6, source-moj-percentage-comparisons

NIST and OpenStax support the quotient, ratio, and per-hundred relationships; the Ministry of Justice report supplies an explicit published comparison of percentage points with relative percent change. The archive example and wording are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

All comparisons appear in words and equations. No answer depends on color, a chart, dragging, or timing. In a nonvisual reading, say “marked files divided by all files” and name each numerator and denominator before the value.

scenes.title: Keep the ratio labels attached
scenes.contentHtml:
Keep the ratio labels attached

A ratio compares two quantities by division. Order matters:

red-to-blue=3 red5 blue≠5 red3 blue.\text{red-to-blue}=\frac{3\ \text{red}}{5\ \text{blue}}\neq\frac{5\ \text{red}}{3\ \text{blue}}.

Multiplying both terms by the same nonzero number creates an equivalent ratio: 3:5=6:103:5=6:10. The compared amounts change, but the quotient does not.

Definition
id definition-proportion

A proportion is an equation stating that two ratios are equal, such as 3/5=x/203/5=x/20.

Solve by following the multiplier: 5→205\to20 multiplies by 4, so 3→x3\to x also multiplies by 4 and x=12x=12. Cross multiplication encodes the same equality, but the multiplier exposes why the answer scales.

Part–whole percentages

“Percent” means per hundred. For a part PP of whole WW,

r=PW,p=100r,P=p100W.r=\frac{P}{W},\qquad p=100r,\qquad P=\frac{p}{100}W.

The whole WW is the reference that represents 100%. A result between 0% and 100% is expected only when 0≤P≤W0\le P\le W; ratios and percent changes can exceed 100% in other contexts.

Percent change

For an ordinary increase or decrease, first check that the starting value OO is positive and that the quantity is measured on a ratio scale with a meaningful zero. Then

percent change=N−OO×100%.\text{percent change}=\frac{N-O}{O}\times100\%.

The denominator is the old value because the question asks how large the change is relative to the starting amount. Always report direction: increase or decrease.

This introductory rule has boundaries:

  • If O=0O=0, division by the starting value is undefined.
  • If O<0O<0, the formula's sign can conflict with ordinary increase/decrease language. For example, −2-2 to −1-1 is an increase, but the quotient is −50%-50\%.
  • On an interval scale with an arbitrary zero, a relative percentage depends on the chosen origin. A rise from 10 ∘C10\ ^\circ\mathrm{C} to 20 ∘C20\ ^\circ\mathrm{C} looks like 100% in Celsius, while the same temperatures in kelvin rise by only about 3.5%.

For these cases, report an absolute difference or use a domain-specific comparison whose reference is justified. Do not force the ordinary percent-change label.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-proportion-equation, claim-percentage-part-whole, claim-percent-change-reference, claim-relative-change-domainsources source-nist-si-chapter8, source-openstax-prealgebra-ch6, source-nist-metrics-measures, source-moj-percentage-comparisons

NIST and OpenStax support the quotient, proportion, and per-hundred relationships. OpenStax and the Ministry of Justice report support use of the starting reference. NIST's measurement-scale discussion supports the meaningful-zero boundary. The organization and examples are original.

scenes.title: Solve by following one multiplier
scenes.contentHtml:
Solve by following one multiplier

A fictional imaging queue processes 42 frames in 6 minutes at a constant rate. How many frames would it process in 15 minutes under that model?

Worked Example
id worked-example-frame-rate

1. Label the ratio. 42 frames/6 min42\text{ frames}/6\text{ min}.

2. State the assumption. The rate is constant, so frames are proportional to time.

3. Find the unit rate. 42/6=7 frames/min42/6=7\text{ frames/min}.

4. Scale. 7 frames/min×15 min=105 frames7\text{ frames/min}\times15\text{ min}=105\text{ frames}.

5. Verify. 105/15=7105/15=7, recovering the original quotient. Also, 15 minutes is 2.5 times 6 minutes, and 42×2.5=10542\times2.5=105.

6. Bound the claim. The result follows only under the stated constant-rate model; the arithmetic does not establish that a real queue remains constant.

Check
id check-proportional-assumption

If the first five minutes include a fixed startup delay, would direct proportionality necessarily remain appropriate? Explain.

Feedback after an attempt: No. A fixed offset can break the “same multiplier” structure. Proportionality is a model to justify, not a default triggered by two numbers.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-proportion-equationsources source-nist-si-chapter8, source-openstax-prealgebra-ch6

The sources support ordered quotient and equivalent-ratio reasoning. The worked example is original and does not claim the fictional rate is empirical.

scenes.title: Percentage points are not percent change
scenes.contentHtml:
Percentage points are not percent change

Suppose a fictional pass fraction rises from 40% to 50%.

Misconception
id misconception-percent-change

Saying “it increased by 10%” silently treats the subtraction as a relative change. The direct subtraction is

50%−40%=10 percentage points.50\%-40\%=10\text{ percentage points}.

The percent increase uses the old 40% as reference:

50%−40%40%×100%=25%.\frac{50\%-40\%}{40\%}\times100\%=25\%.

A reverse check confirms it: 40%×1.25=50%40\%\times1.25=50\%.

The reference-first question is: “Ten is what fraction of the starting forty?” If no starting value is named, “percent change” is incomplete.

Source Note
claims claim-percent-change-reference, claim-percentage-point-differencesources source-openstax-prealgebra-ch6, source-moj-percentage-comparisons

OpenStax supports change relative to the original amount, and the Ministry of Justice report explicitly distinguishes a percentage-point difference from relative percentage change. The example and repair are original.

scenes.title: Find the reference before calculating
scenes.contentHtml:
Find the reference before calculating

For each task, write labels → reference → equation → check. Complete all four attempts before continuing to the feedback section.

Check
id check-practice-equivalent-ratio

1. Equivalent ratio: Complete 8:12=x:308:12=x:30 and verify the result by simplifying both ratios.

Check
id check-practice-find-part

2. Find a part: What is 18% of 250? Use the part-whole range as a plausibility check.

Check
id check-practice-find-whole

3. Find the whole: Thirty-six objects are 24% of a collection. Find the collection size and reverse the calculation.

Check
id check-practice-percent-decrease

4. Percent decrease: A fictional count falls from 80 to 62. Find the percentage decrease and name its denominator.

Feedback after all four attempts

  1. 12→3012\to30 multiplies by 2.5, so x=20x=20. Both 8:128:12 and 20:3020:30 simplify to 2:32:3.
  2. 0.18(250)=450.18(250)=45. The result is below the whole because the percentage is below 100% and the part is constrained to lie within the whole.
  3. 36=0.24W36=0.24W, so W=150W=150. Reverse: 0.24(150)=360.24(150)=36.
  4. The decrease is 18, referenced to the positive starting value 80: 18/80=0.225=22.5%18/80=0.225=22.5\%. Dividing by 62 would use the new value and answer a different question.

Recovery route

  • Ratio reversed: restore verbal labels before numbers.
  • Proportion gives an implausible direction: identify the multiplier on both quantities.
  • Part exceeds whole when percentage is below 100%: inspect multiplication versus division.
  • Percent-change denominator uncertain: write “change relative to the positive starting value.”
  • Percentage points confused with percent: compute both and label them.
Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-proportion-equation, claim-percentage-part-whole, claim-percent-change-reference, claim-percentage-point-differencesources source-nist-si-chapter8, source-openstax-prealgebra-ch6, source-moj-percentage-comparisons

The sources support the quotient, proportion, per-hundred, starting-reference, and point-versus-relative-change relationships. All practice items, feedback, and remediation are original.

scenes.title: Audit a changing mixture record
scenes.contentHtml:
Audit a changing mixture record

Check
id check-transfer-mixture-record

A fictional materials record describes 18 blue beads and 42 white beads. After 12 blue beads are added, no beads are removed.

Before reading the feedback, determine:

  1. the original blue-to-white ratio;
  2. the original blue percentage of all beads;
  3. the new blue percentage;
  4. the percentage-point change and relative percent change in the blue share; and
  5. one reverse or denominator check.

Feedback after a complete attempt

Original ratio: 18:42=3:718:42=3:7. Original total: 60, so blue share is 18/60=30%18/60=30\%.

New blue count: 30; new total: 72. New blue share is 30/72=5/12≈41.67%30/72=5/12\approx41.67\%.

Percentage-point increase: 41.67−30=11.6741.67-30=11.67 points. Relative increase in the blue share:

41.67%−30%30%×100%≈38.89%.\frac{41.67\%-30\%}{30\%}\times100\%\approx38.89\%.

Check: a 38.89% increase applied to 30% gives about 41.67%. If you kept 60 as the new denominator, you failed to update the whole when objects were added.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-percentage-part-whole, claim-percent-change-reference, claim-percentage-point-differencesources source-nist-si-chapter8, source-openstax-prealgebra-ch6, source-moj-percentage-comparisons

The sources support the ratio, part-whole, and two change comparisons. The bead record, numbers, solution, and checks are fictional original material.

scenes.title: Reconstruct the reference-first method
scenes.contentHtml:
Reconstruct the reference-first method

  1. Attach labels and preserve ratio order.
  2. State why proportional scaling is justified.
  3. Name the whole or positive starting value that supplies the denominator.
  4. Check that a relative comparison has a meaningful zero; otherwise choose an absolute or domain-specific comparison.
  5. Calculate with a fraction before converting to percent.
  6. Reverse, substitute, or bound the answer.
Check
id check-synthesis-reference-first

Answer before reading the feedback:

  • Why are 3:53:5 and 6:106:10 equivalent?
  • What assumption makes a proportion appropriate?
  • In ordinary percent change, why is the old value the denominator?
  • Why is “100% increase from 10 ∘C10\ ^\circ\mathrm{C} to 20 ∘C20\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}” misleading?
  • How do percentage points differ from relative percent change?

Feedback after an attempt: The first ratio is multiplied term-by-term by 2, so its quotient is unchanged. A proportion needs a justified constant multiplier or constant rate. Ordinary percent change compares the difference with a positive starting value. Celsius has an arbitrary zero, so ratios of Celsius readings change when the origin changes. Percentage points subtract two percentages; relative percent change divides their difference by the starting percentage.

Then complete assessment.json. If ratio order fails, return to “Keep the ratio labels attached.” If the wrong denominator appears, redo the mixture transfer with every whole written explicitly. If arithmetic is correct but the comparison is unlabeled, add “percentage points” or “percent increase/decrease” before retrying. If the boundary item fails, return to the percent-change boundaries and ask whether the starting value is positive and its zero is meaningful.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-proportion-equation, claim-percentage-part-whole, claim-percent-change-reference, claim-percentage-point-difference, claim-relative-change-domainsources source-nist-si-chapter8, source-openstax-prealgebra-ch6, source-nist-metrics-measures, source-moj-percentage-comparisons

The sources support the quotient, proportion, percentage, point-versus-relative-change, change-reference, and measurement-scale boundaries summarized here. The reference-first procedure and retrieval wording are original and make no claim of measured effectiveness.

aiAssistance.disclosure: VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable. OpenAI Codex, model gpt-5.6-sol, authored, researched, edited, and packaged this lesson in run 704652be-a413-411c-9af3-33220ef604a9. The digest covers discloseable material task instructions, not hidden system or provider instructions.

assessment.title: Ratios, proportions, and percentages mastery gate

items.prompt: A box has 12 large and 18 small objects. What is the large-to-small ratio in simplest form?

answer.reasoning: The requested order is large then small; divide both terms by 6.

items.prompt: Solve 7/9 = x/45 and verify your value by substitution.

answer.summary: x = 35, and substitution restores the original ratio.
answer.reasoning: The denominator is multiplied by 5, so the numerator is also multiplied by 5; 35/45 simplifies to 7/9.

items.prompt: Forty-two records are 28% of an archive. Find the archive size and show a reverse check.

answer.reasoning: The archive is the 100% reference, so divide the part by the decimal percentage.

items.prompt: A count rises from 64 to 80. Enter the percent increase.

answer.reasoning: The change is 16 and the old-value reference is 64; 16/64 = 0.25.
commonErrors: Reporting the change of 16 as a percentage

items.prompt: A share falls from 50% to 35%. Report both the percentage-point decrease and the relative percent decrease, naming each reference.

answer.correct: The decrease is 15 percentage points. Relative to the starting 50%, it is 15/50 = 30%.
answer.summary: 15 percentage points and a 30% relative decrease.
answer.reasoning: Subtraction compares positions on the percentage scale; division by the starting share measures relative change.
acceptableResponses: 15 points and a 30 percent decrease relative to the starting 50 percent

items.prompt: A report says that a temperature rise from 10 °C to 20 °C is a 100% increase. Which response best challenges that claim?

options.text: The claim is valid because 20 is twice 10.
The claim is misleading because Celsius has an arbitrary zero; report the 10 °C difference or justify another scale and reference.
Any increase of 10 degrees is automatically 100%.
Percent change should divide by the new value, so the increase is 50%.

answer.summary: Ordinary relative percent change is not stable on the arbitrary-origin Celsius scale.
answer.reasoning: The Celsius zero is not a meaningful absence of temperature. Shifting the origin to kelvin changes the numerical ratio, so the safe direct statement is an increase of 10 Celsius degrees unless a domain-specific relative measure is justified.
commonErrors: Treating any displayed zero as a meaningful zero

items.prompt: A fictional collection initially has 24 tagged and 56 untagged items. Sixteen tagged items are added and none removed. Find the initial tagged-to-untagged ratio, initial and new tagged percentages, percentage-point change, and relative percent change in tagged share. Show denominators and one reverse check.

answer.correct: Initial ratio 24:56 = 3:7. Initial share 24/80 = 30%. New share 40/96 = 41.666...%. Increase 11.666... percentage points; relative increase 11.666.../30 × 100 = 38.888...%. Check: 30% × 1.38888... = 41.666...%.
answer.summary: 3:7; 30%; about 41.67%; about 11.67 points; about 38.89% relative increase.
answer.reasoning: Adding tagged items changes both the tagged numerator and total denominator. Percentage points subtract shares; relative change divides by the starting share.
commonErrors: Using the new share as percent-change denominator

criteria.description: Applies the same factor to both terms and obtains 35.
Substitutes 35 and verifies that 35/45 simplifies to 7/9.
Divides the change by the old value and obtains 25%.
Uses the old 50% reference to obtain a 30% decrease.
Identifies the arbitrary Celsius zero and selects an absolute or justified domain-specific comparison.
Finds and simplifies the initial ordered ratio.
Uses totals 80 and 96 for the two shares.
Labels about 11.67 points and 38.89% relative increase correctly.

claims.statement: A ratio is an ordered comparison of two quantities by division, and multiplying both terms by the same nonzero factor preserves the quotient.
claims.scope: Ordered ratios and equivalent ratios in introductory quantitative reasoning.
claims.uncertainty: Whether two changing quantities are proportional requires a separate contextual assumption.

claims.statement: A proportion is an equation asserting that two ratios are equal; a proposed solution can be verified by substitution.
claims.uncertainty: An equation can be solved algebraically even when a real context does not justify proportional scaling.

claims.statement: A percentage expresses a ratio per hundred, so a part-whole percentage must name the whole that represents 100 percent.
claims.scope: Introductory part-whole percentages and their fractional and decimal forms.
claims.uncertainty: Percentages above 100 percent are possible when the numerator is not constrained to be a subset of the named whole.

claims.statement: Relative percent change compares the difference between new and old values with the old value.
claims.scope: Ordinary increase or decrease from a positive starting reference.
claims.uncertainty: The reference and comparison direction must be named; the separate meaningful-zero boundary also applies.

claims.statement: A percentage-point change is the arithmetic difference between two percentages and is distinct from relative percent change.
claims.scope: Direct comparison of two values already expressed as percentages.
claims.uncertainty: A point difference and a relative percentage answer different questions and must be labelled.

claims.statement: Ordinary percent increase or decrease is meaningful for a positive starting value on a ratio scale with a meaningful zero; zero, negative, and arbitrary-origin baselines require different handling.
claims.scope: Introductory ordinary percent change, not specialized signed, logarithmic, index, or domain-specific change measures.
claims.uncertainty: A discipline may define another relative-change convention when its quantities or decisions require one.

terms.definition: An ordered comparison of two quantities by division.

An equation asserting that two ratios are equal, for example a/b = c/d. This differs from the statistical sense of proportion as a part-to-whole fraction between 0 and 1, introduced in later data-analysis lessons.
A ratio expressed per hundred relative to a named whole or reference quantity.
For an ordinary positive ratio-scale baseline, the change divided by the starting value, expressed as a percentage and labelled as an increase or decrease.
The arithmetic difference between two percentages, distinct from their relative percent change.
The whole, baseline, or starting value used as the denominator that gives a ratio or percentage its meaning.
A numerical scale with a meaningful zero, so ratios of values can have a stable interpretation.
A numerical scale on which differences are meaningful but the zero point is arbitrary, so ratios of displayed values depend on the chosen origin.

The digest identifies /tmp/codex-packager-prompt-03-03.md; it excludes unavailable hidden instructions. The two isolated advisory reviews are disclosed in reviews/.

Post-review finalization

A fresh finalizer made the single post-review content revision, completed the accessibility-and-rights audit, and wrote adjudication.json in run codex-finalizer-PREM-QNT-003-D0E024C6-297A-4960-91F3-100AD958FA6A. Its material-instructions digest is sha256:3c91af3a17d0ad76c37bebf1717cdfcea9dd4d9b2c30989d2adcc15d732ed193. The adjudication intentionally leaves the four runtime identity fields as RUNTIME-STAMPED for the accountable operator to replace from the runtime banner.

Originality, sources, and rights

The reference-first structure, archive opening, queue example, pass-fraction misconception, bead transfer, all values, explanations, feedback, recovery routes, and assessment content are original. All settings are fictional. NIST Chapter 8 supports quotient relations; the first-edition OpenStax Prealgebra supports proportions, per-hundred percentages, and change from an original amount; NIST's measurement-scale page supports the meaningful-zero boundary; and the UK Ministry of Justice report supplies a published percentage-point versus relative-change distinction. These sources are used only for facts. No source wording, table, image, data, example, or sequence is copied or adapted.

The displayed licences, terms routes, and public access conditions for NIST, OpenStax, and GOV.UK were checked before access on 2026-07-22. No source-specific agent prohibition, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. No third-party assets, datasets, personal data, or patient material are included.

This mathematical lesson provides no clinical advice and makes no claim of review, approval, publication, measured effectiveness, credit, or competence.
